section.title: Priests and Prophets
section.sectionRationale: This chapter contrasts Israel's God-given offices, the priest and the prophet, with the detestable divination of the nations, and climaxes in the LORD's promise to raise up a prophet like Moses, a hinge text the apostles later applied directly to Jesus.
section.thesis: Because Israel must not seek hidden knowledge through pagan divination, the LORD provides two true channels of access to Himself: the Levitical priesthood, whose only inheritance is the LORD, and, above all, a prophet like Moses whom the New Testament identifies as Jesus Christ, tested by whether his word comes true.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the priests' portion; the ban on divination and the detestable practices of the nations; the promise of a prophet like Moses; and the test for recognizing a true prophet's word.

units.label: The priests' portion
units.note: the LORD Himself is their inheritance

verses.text: The Levitical priests, indeed the whole tribe of Levi, shall have no portion or inheritance with Israel. They are to eat the offerings made by fire to the LORD; that is their inheritance.
Although they have no inheritance among their brothers, the LORD is their inheritance, as He promised them.
This shall be the priests' share from the people who offer a sacrifice, whether a bull or a sheep: the priests are to be given the shoulder, the jowls, and the stomach.
You are to give them the firstfruits of your grain, new wine, and oil, and the first wool sheared from your flock.
For the LORD your God has chosen Levi and his sons out of all your tribes to stand and minister in His name for all time.
Now if a Levite moves from any town of residence throughout Israel and comes in all earnestness to the place the LORD will choose,
then he shall serve in the name of the LORD his God like all his fellow Levites who stand there before the LORD.
They shall eat equal portions, even though he has received money from the sale of his father's estate.

units.label: No divination, only the LORD
units.note: the detestable practices of the nations

verses.text: When you enter the land that the LORD your God is giving you, do not imitate the detestable ways of the nations there.
Let no one be found among you who sacrifices his son or daughter in the fire, practices divination or conjury, interprets omens, practices sorcery,
casts spells, consults a medium or spiritist, or inquires of the dead.
For whoever does these things is {{toebah|detestable}} to the LORD. And because of these detestable things, the LORD your God is driving out the nations before you.
You must be {{tamim|blameless}} before the LORD your God.
Though these nations, which you will dispossess, listen to conjurers and diviners, the LORD your God has not permitted you to do so.

units.label: A prophet like Moses
units.note: the LORD's provision for the fear at Horeb

verses.text: The LORD your God will raise up for you a {{navi|prophet}} like me from among your brothers. You must listen to him.

This is what you asked of the LORD your God at Horeb on the day of the assembly, when you said, "Let us not hear the voice of the LORD our God or see this great fire anymore, so that we will not die!"
Then the LORD said to me, "They have spoken well.
I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers. I will put My words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him.
And I will hold accountable anyone who does not listen to My words that the prophet speaks in My name.
But if any prophet dares to speak a message in My name that I have not commanded him to speak, or to speak in the name of other gods, that prophet must be put to death."
You may ask in your heart, "How can we recognize a message that the LORD has not spoken?"

When a prophet speaks in the name of the LORD and the message does not come to pass or come true, that is a message the LORD has not spoken. The prophet has spoken presumptuously. Do not be afraid of him.

navi.gloss: A prophet, one who speaks on behalf of another. A navi does not originate a message but delivers exactly what God has put in his mouth. The office finds its perfect and final fulfillment in Christ, the ultimate Prophet, Acts 3:22-23.

toebah.gloss: Detestable, an abomination, something that violates the LORD's character and order. Applied here to child sacrifice and every form of divination, the very practices the surrounding nations built their religion on.

tamim.gloss: Blameless, complete, whole-hearted. Israel is called to undivided loyalty to the LORD rather than hedging their trust with pagan divination on the side.

basic.context: Moses turns from land and leadership laws to two offices that mediate God's presence to Israel: the priesthood, who serve at the altar with no inheritance but the LORD Himself, and prophecy, God's answer to the divination and sorcery that surrounded Israel in Canaan. The chapter closes with a stunning promise: God will raise up a prophet like Moses.
basic.hermeneutics: This is genuinely about ancient Israel's priests and its coming prophetic office, but Peter and Stephen both treat the prophet like Moses as a direct prophecy of Jesus, not merely a loose pattern (Acts 3:22-23, 7:37). The ban on occult practices also speaks honestly into today's forms of the same temptation, seeking hidden knowledge or power apart from God's revealed word.
basic.originalLanguages: The navi, prophet, does not invent a message but speaks only what God puts in his mouth. Israel is called to be tamim, blameless or whole-hearted, rather than divided between the LORD and pagan divination. The forbidden practices are toebah, a strong word for whatever violates God's order and character.
basic.typology: Moses himself, the mediator who spoke with God directly, becomes the pattern for every true prophet after him, and supremely for Christ, who speaks the very words of God perfectly and finally, Hebrews 1:1-2.
basic.christ: Verse 15's promised prophet is explicitly applied to Jesus in Acts 3:22-23 and 7:37: he is the greater Moses, whose words carry the full authority of God and whose rejection brings judgment, verse 19.
basic.god: God does not leave His people to grope after hidden knowledge through divination; He speaks plainly. He provides for their real needs, priests to mediate access and a prophet to speak His words directly, so they need not fear or manipulate unseen powers.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 3:22-23 and 7:37 apply the prophet like Moses directly to Jesus. Hebrews 1:1-2 says God has spoken finally by His Son. 1 Corinthians 10:20 warns that pagan sacrifices are offered to demons, not empty nothings.
basic.oneStory: From the priesthood's atoning access and the prophet's authoritative word, the whole Bible moves toward the one who is final Prophet, Priest, and King, Jesus Christ, in whom God's speech to His people reaches its fullness.

context.literary: This chapter completes the section on Israel's leaders, judges, king, priests, and prophets, begun in 16:18, moving from civil leadership to religious mediation, and ends the unit with the prophetic office that anticipates Christ.
context.historical: Israel was surrounded by nations whose religion depended on divination and necromancy. God gives His people a wholly different way of knowing His will, through appointed priests and a true prophetic word, tested by whether it comes to pass.
context.canonical: This chapter grounds the entire prophetic tradition of the Old Testament and becomes a key messianic text, quoted at Pentecost's aftermath by Peter and by Stephen before the Sanhedrin to identify Jesus as the promised prophet.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To secure Israel's exclusive dependence on the LORD for guidance, refusing the occult practices of the nations, and to establish a true prophetic office through which God would keep speaking, climaxing in one prophet like Moses.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific priestly provisions and the test for a true prophet describe Israel's old covenant arrangements; the enduring principle, that God's people seek Him through His appointed means and revealed word rather than occult practices, still applies, and the prophet like Moses is directly prescriptive as messianic prophecy fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Is Deuteronomy 18:15-19 about a single individual or an ongoing prophetic succession?

views.label: Primarily an ongoing succession
views.case: The near context concerns a continuing line of true prophets replacing pagan divination, since Israel would need prophetic guidance for centuries.

views.label: A climactic, singular fulfillment
views.case: Peter (Acts 3:22-23) and Stephen (Acts 7:37) apply the text pointedly to Jesus alone as the definitive, greater Moses, suggesting the whole line of prophets was itself pointing toward one final Prophet.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: God raised up many true prophets, and that whole succession finds its terminus and fulfillment in Jesus, the Prophet like Moses in the fullest sense.

study.originalLanguages: The navi, prophet, does not invent a message but speaks only what God puts in his mouth, verse 18. Israel is called to be tamim, blameless or whole-hearted, rather than divided between the LORD and pagan divination, verse 13. The forbidden practices of verses 10-11 are toebah, detestable, a strong word for whatever violates God's order and character.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, the greater Moses, who speaks God's very words with final authority, Hebrews 1:1-2.

typology.type: The Levitical priesthood with no inheritance but the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ our great high priest, whose portion is the Father, and who secures believers' eternal inheritance in Him.

study.christ: Acts 3:22-23 quotes this passage directly of Jesus: 'The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own people... Anyone who does not listen to him will be completely cut off from among his people.' Jesus is the final, authoritative Word of God, Hebrews 1:1-2, whose message never fails, unlike the false prophets warned against in verses 20-22.
study.god: God refuses to let His people wander after hidden powers; He speaks clearly and provides faithful mediators. He also holds prophets accountable: a true word from Him always comes to pass, and He will judge whoever speaks falsely in His name.

crossReferences.note: Peter applies the prophet like Moses directly to the risen Jesus.
Stephen cites the same prophecy before the Sanhedrin.
God has spoken finally and fully through His Son.
Paul warns that pagan sacrifices, like the divination banned here, are dealings with demons.

study.oneStory: God provides His people true access to Himself, priesthood and prophetic word, rather than the counterfeit access of divination. That prophetic line climaxes in Jesus, the Prophet like Moses, whose word is final and who must be heeded above every other voice.

sermon.title: Jesus Vs. the Occult
sermon.why: Piper works directly through Deuteronomy 18:9-14 on the ban on occult practices and then to verses 15-19, explicitly naming Jesus as the fulfillment of the prophet like Moses through Acts 3:22-23, making it a strong match for this chapter's full argument.

quiz.q: What is given as the priests' true inheritance in this chapter?

options.text: The LORD Himself, rather than a portion of land like the other tribes.
options.note: Verses 1-2 explicitly state that the LORD is Levi's inheritance.

options.text: A double portion of every sacrifice offered by the people.
options.note: The priests do receive a real share of the offerings, verses 3-4, but that is not what the text calls their 'inheritance'; that term is reserved for the LORD Himself.

options.text: Land taken from the Canaanite nations.
options.note: The Levites specifically receive no land inheritance among their brothers, verse 1.

options.text: Nothing at all; they were left to fend for themselves.
options.note: Verses 3-5 describe real, commanded provision from the offerings and firstfruits.

quiz.q: According to this chapter, what practices does the LORD forbid Israel from imitating?

options.text: Child sacrifice, divination, sorcery, and consulting mediums or the dead.
options.note: Verses 10-11 list these detestable practices in detail.

options.text: Only child sacrifice.
options.note: Child sacrifice heads the list, but verses 10-11 name several other occult practices as well.

options.text: Eating certain foods.
options.note: That is not the subject of this passage; see chapter 14 for dietary laws.

options.text: Nothing; the chapter gives no such prohibition.
options.note: Verses 9-12 give an explicit, detailed prohibition.

quiz.q: Why would God raise up a prophet like Moses, according to verse 16?

options.text: Because the people themselves asked at Horeb not to hear God's voice directly, fearing they would die.
options.note: Verse 16 recalls the people's own request at Horeb for a mediator.

options.text: Because Moses requested it for himself before he died.
options.note: The origin is the people's fear at Horeb, not a personal request from Moses.

options.text: Because the priests were unable to teach the law.
options.note: The priesthood's function is addressed earlier; the prophet answers the people's fear of hearing God directly.

options.text: For no stated reason.
options.note: Verse 16 gives an explicit reason rooted in Israel's own request at Horeb.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament identify the prophet like Moses?

options.text: As Jesus Christ, quoted directly by Peter in Acts 3:22-23.
options.note: Peter applies this prophecy explicitly to the risen Christ.

options.text: As John the Baptist.
options.note: John pointed to Christ, but the New Testament reserves this specific title for Jesus Himself.

options.text: As an unfulfilled promise still awaited.
options.note: Acts 3 treats the prophecy as already fulfilled in Jesus.

options.text: The New Testament never addresses this prophecy.
options.note: Acts 3:22-23 and 7:37 both directly cite it.

quiz.q: What is the test for whether a prophet has truly spoken from the LORD?

options.text: Whether the message comes to pass or comes true.
options.note: Verse 22 gives this explicit test.

options.text: Whether the prophet is popular with the people.
options.note: Popularity is never given as the test; fulfillment is.

options.text: Whether the prophet performs miracles.
options.note: Miracles are not the test given here; the fulfillment of the specific word is.

options.text: There is no test given.
options.note: Verse 22 gives an explicit and clear test.

questions: Israel was tempted to seek hidden knowledge through divination rather than trust God's revealed word. Where are you tempted to look for guidance outside of what God has actually said?
The Levites' portion was the LORD Himself, not land. What would change in your life if you treasured God Himself above the things He gives?
God promised a prophet whose words carried His full authority, fulfilled in Jesus. How seriously do you take the words of Christ compared with other voices competing for your trust?
The people at Horeb were too afraid to hear God's voice directly and asked for a mediator. How does having Christ as your mediator change your posture toward God, fear or confidence?
A true prophet's words always came to pass. Where do you need to test the promises you are relying on against what God has actually said in Scripture?

translationNotes.note: The list of forbidden practices, divination, conjury, omens, sorcery, spells, mediums, spiritists, necromancy, uses several overlapping Hebrew terms for occult activity; translators vary in exact English equivalents, but the cumulative sense is a total ban on seeking hidden knowledge from anyone but the LORD.

'A prophet like me' can be read collectively, an ongoing line of prophets, or singularly, one final prophet; the New Testament reads it climactically of Christ without denying the intervening line of true prophets.
'I will hold accountable' translates a Hebrew idiom of personal reckoning, underscoring that rejecting God's true spokesman is treated as rejecting God Himself.
